opp.ais_why:
• Zycus has logged AI importance as 'Don't Know' for Revvity, meaning the rep has not heard the buyer articulate any AI mandate. ANA appears in the Merlin product line, but that is vendor-tagged rather than buyer-requested. The opportunity name itself, 'S2P RFP', signals a traditional source-to-pay evaluation, and no Gen AI commentary or Merlin Intake conversation has been captured anywhere on the record.
• On the Oct 17 discovery call with Markus Vogel, Arun Pandey and Nikhil Sahasrabuddhe (31 minutes), neither AI, Merlin, GenAI nor autonomous negotiation came up once from the buyer side. Vogel kept the conversation on integration, telling Zycus the platform has to work with "ECC, S4 HANA and Dynamics 365" simultaneously. The priorities were supplier inventory visibility, indirect spend governance, and implementation cost.
• Action items reinforce a classical evaluation: API feasibility, supplier portal questions, RFP timeline flexibility, and implementation partner pricing ranges. No AI champion has surfaced. With one substantive call on the books and the buyer parked at 'Don't Know' on AI, this is a wait-and-see posture rather than a Resistant deal, but there is nothing yet to lift it above the lower band.

metrics.narrative: Revvity quantified the indirect addressable spend at $120-130 million with only 50-60% currently under procurement governance, surfacing ~$50-65M of unmanaged maverick spend as the primary pain (Nikhil, 17 Oct 2025). The buyer explicitly seeks 'complete control on overall indirect spend happening today in a very chaotic manner', improved spend analytics (currently lacking), and tools to capture cost pilferages and leakage that are invisible today. Nikhil listed top KPIs as: (1) control chaotic indirect spend, (2) visibility and analytics, (3) reduce pilferages/leakages, and (4) market intelligence on POs and contracts — framing this as 'a huge list… we want to start somewhere' (17 Oct 2025). No quantified savings target or ROI was stated, but the scale of unmanaged spend and the buyer's explicit pain points establish a clear, quantified business problem. The gap is converting this into a formal, signed business case with ROI — Markus noted they are building a supplier-agnostic business case with KPMG for S/4 and broader procurement, and declined Zycus help to 'do a reality check… be realistic' vs 'extremely lofty business cases' (17 Oct 2025). Recommended move: co-author a joint value case quantifying savings on the $50-65M unmanaged bucket (tail-spend consolidation, contract compliance, analytics-driven sourcing) and present it to the Economic Buyer.

champion.narrative: No strong champion is identified. Nikhil Sahasrabuddhe (global indirect procurement lead, joined Revvity Dec 2024, ~10 months tenure as of 17 Oct 2025) is the closest: he led the 17 Oct 2025 Q&A call, articulated the pain and KPIs, and has Zycus familiarity ('I implemented Zycus in one of my early organizations, fairly proud of Zycus and its solutions' — 17 Oct 2025). However, his engagement since the call is absent (no recorded activity in 8 months), and he explicitly stated the RFP timeline is 'too aspirational' and 'we don't need to rush', signaling he is not driving urgency. His role (indirect lead, no executive title, no EB/DM designation in SF) limits his ability to drive commercial and budget decisions. Markus Vogel (Digital Transformation Leader, S/4 program lead) is a key influencer with IT/process authority and articulated the ERP integration requirements, but he is focused on S/4 HANA (led by KPMG) and was cautious about locking an SI partner for S2P, suggesting his sponsorship is conditional. Kevin Marcell (Senior Director Strategic Sourcing, contact role in SF but absent from the 17 Oct call) is the direct procurement lead and likely a decision influencer for the sourcing/direct modules, but he has not engaged. Scott Bichan (VP Lifecycle Quality Operations, on the call) is senior but peripheral (quality, not procurement core). No one on the call demonstrated advocacy (introductions to the EB, pushing the timeline, competitive defense, sharing internal docs). The champion status is 'developing at best' — Nikhil is a warm, knowledgeable contact but not an active advocate, and no executive sponsor has surfaced. The risk: an 8-month silence suggests Nikhil (or the broader team) is not championing Zycus internally, and the deal may be stalled or de-prioritized. Recommended immediate move: re-engage Nikhil directly to understand (1) whether the S2P initiative is still a 2026 priority (per the 04 May Next Step), (2) his internal advocacy status, (3) who is championing the project at the executive level, and (4) secure an intro to that person or to Kevin (direct lead), within 7 days.

competition.narrative: Two competitors were named explicitly on the 17 Oct 2025 Q&A call, in passing, as reference points for market intelligence capability, not as active RFP participants: Coupa and SAP Ariba. Nikhil stated: 'the kind of intelligence that tools like yours and Coupa and Arivas provide in terms of market intelligence plus the inner interests… I think it's a huge list' (17 Oct 2025). This phrasing ('tools like yours and Coupa and Ariba') suggests Nikhil is aware of these vendors and views them as category peers to Zycus, but does NOT confirm they are on the shortlist. No other competitor was mentioned on the call, and the Salesforce Competitor field is empty (unknown per ground truth). The RFP context (Markus: 'fairly extensive RFP… we wanted to cover all our bases' and Nikhil: 'Arun should be able to come back with updated timeline next week') implies a formal multi-vendor evaluation, but the shortlist composition is unconfirmed. The competitive landscape is inferred as: (1) Coupa — likely a shortlist peer, strong S2P brand, but Nikhil's phrasing was neutral (listed alongside Zycus and Ariba as category examples, not as a preferred choice); (2) SAP Ariba — natural fit given Revvity's SAP ECC footprint and S/4 HANA migration, but Zycus positioned a wedge on the 17 Oct call (Srishti: 'we are one of only two S2P players with certified S/4 HANA adapters', and John: 'PWC made us wait for S/4 cutover, don't recommend that… integrate with ECC first, update adapters later when S/4 is live, much wiser' — 17 Oct 2025). This positions Zycus as more flexible/pragmatic than Ariba's tightly-coupled SAP approach. No incumbent S2P platform was mentioned (Nikhil: 'there's nothing really called a source to pay system' today). The competitive read is partial because the shortlist is unconfirmed and no competitive bake-off, demo schedule, or feature comparison has occurred. The 8-month silence since the Q&A call raises the risk that another vendor has engaged more aggressively or that the RFP itself is stalled/de-prioritized. Recommended move: re-engage to confirm (1) shortlist composition, (2) how Zycus is positioned vs Coupa/Ariba, (3) whether demos or use-case sessions are scheduled, and (4) any competitive intel from Nikhil or Kevin on what other vendors are emphasizing, within 14 days.

sources: Avoma Q&A call 17 Oct 2025, Nikhil: 'chaotic spend, 40-50% unmanaged, worst in automation, nothing from source to pay, no POs, all manual, no analytics, no visibility, cost pilferages invisible'
Avoma Q&A call 17 Oct 2025, Nikhil: 'we want complete control… visibility… analytics we completely lack… pilferages we can't capture'
Avoma Q&A call 17 Oct 2025, Markus/Madhil: 'direct sourcing portal missing, supplier collaboration manual, no inventory visibility, no unified portal'

identify_pain.narrative: Revvity articulated acute, multi-dimensional procurement pain across indirect and direct. On indirect (Nikhil's domain, ~$120-130M addressable): (1) 'chaotic' spend with 40-50% maverick buying (unmanaged), (2) zero automation ('the worst in terms of automation… nothing really in place from source to pay… we don't create POs… it's all manual'), (3) no spend visibility or analytics, (4) no ability to enforce contract compliance post-signature (contracts in Agiloft, but no link to purchasing to prevent duplicate suppliers or off-contract buys), and (5) cost pilferages/leakages invisible due to lack of tooling. Nikhil stated: 'we want complete control on overall indirect spend happening today in a very chaotic manner… once we have visibility, look at tools which help us do better analytics which we completely lack today… situations where cost pilferages… there's no way we are able to capture that' (17 Oct 2025). On direct (Kevin's domain, SAP ECC-based): the execution layer (MRP, PO creation) exists in ECC, but the sourcing portal is missing, and supplier collaboration is manual — no automated forecast sharing, no supplier inventory visibility, no unified portal for PO/catalog/communication (Markus and Madhil, 17 Oct 2025). The pain owners are clear: Nikhil (indirect procurement lead) owns the indirect chaos; Kevin (Senior Director Strategic Sourcing, not on the call) and Markus (digital transformation, on the call) own the direct/supplier-collaboration gap. The compelling event is the S/4 HANA migration (pilot end 2026/Q1 2027): Revvity needs an S2P platform that integrates with ECC now, Dynamics 365 (another BU), and S/4 later, making 'now' the window to select and implement before the ERP cutover adds complexity (Markus, 17 Oct 2025). The pain is confirmed and quantified; the next move is to map these pains to a joint ROI model (savings on maverick spend, contract compliance, supplier collaboration efficiency) and present it to the Economic Buyer.

paper_process.narrative: Contracting mechanics are partially visible but not formalized. Nikhil mentioned Revvity recently implemented Agiloft CLM (went live 'a couple months back' as of 17 Oct 2025, so likely ~Aug 2025), with 'legal as gatekeepers, indirect procurement as contract managers, and IT owning tech support' — indicating a structured CLM governance process is now in place. The buyer wants the S2P platform to integrate with Agiloft to enforce contract compliance at purchase time (preventing maverick buying by surfacing existing contracts before new PO creation), which Zycus confirmed as feasible. No mention of MSA, SOW, or Order Form mechanics for the Zycus deal itself, nor of InfoSec review, legal redlines, or procurement channel (direct vs through a partner). Markus noted they have 'not locked an implementation partner yet' and are open to 'big ones and small ones', with KPMG currently running the S/4 transformation but not confirmed for S2P implementation — this affects SOW scoping. The S/4 HANA pilot timeline (end 2026 or Q1 2027) is a parallel forcing function: the S2P platform must be live and integrated with ECC before the S/4 cutover, or risk dual-integration complexity. No current contract expiration or renewal lever was mentioned. The gap: no visibility into Revvity's procurement/legal process for vendor contracts, no InfoSec questionnaire status, and no implementation partner locked (which blocks accurate SOW and cost estimate per John's 17 Oct comment). Recommended move: ask Nikhil for the standard vendor contracting process (legal review SLA, InfoSec requirements, PO vs MSA threshold) and propose a 3-party scoping workshop (Zycus, Revvity, and a named SI partner) to align on implementation cost and SOW, within 21 days.

decision_process.narrative: The decision process is RFP-driven with vendor shortlisting underway as of 17 Oct 2025, but the downstream stages (demos, commercials, EB presentation, contracting) and their timeline are unclear. Nikhil stated on 17 Oct 2025 that the original RFP timeline was 'too aspirational' and they 'should have some flexibility… don't really need to rush… Arun should be able to come back to you next week' with updated dates — but no updated timeline has been recorded since. The Q&A call sequence shows Revvity gathering deep functional requirements (the 17 Oct call was a technical deep-dive with procurement, IT, and operations leads), implying shortlist vendors were given the same Q&A session. Markus mentioned KPMG is building a supplier-agnostic business case, suggesting the EB review of that case is a gate before vendor selection. decision_criteria.narrative: The RFP itself (mentioned repeatedly on the 17 Oct 2025 call) defines the formal decision criteria, but the criteria were not detailed on the Q&A call. Markus stated the RFP is 'fairly extensive… we wanted to cover all our bases… get as much detail as possible to understand how licensing works, by module, for different functionalities, and make an informed decision what makes sense for Revvity to implement' (17 Oct 2025). Confirmed evaluation priorities inferred from buyer questions: (1) ERP integration flexibility — must integrate with the relevant stakeholder (current), Dynamics 365 (another BU), and future S/4 HANA (pilot end 2026/Q1 2027); (2) supplier collaboration at scale (2,000+ addressable suppliers, inventory visibility for top 10-50 direct suppliers, forecast sharing, PO/catalog management on one portal); (3) contract governance and spend visibility (integrate with the relevant stakeholder to enforce contracts at purchase time); (4) global deployment for all BUs; (5) implementation cost and partner flexibility (Markus hesitant to lock KPMG, wants options). The buyer also asked about external market benchmarking, which Zycus positioned as available via third-party data. What is missing: formal scoring/weighting of criteria, use-case test scenarios, and whether AI/analytics capability (our ANA product is in scope per SF Products field) is a differentiator or table-stakes. The next move is to request the full RFP scoring rubric and decision matrix, and map our strengths (certified S/4 adapter, supplier portal scale, Agiloft integration, modular licensing) to their weights.

ai_fit_signal.summary: AI fit signal is unclear. AIS fields are null in Salesforce (no AIS_Score, AIS_Status, or AIS_Why recorded), so there is no prior rep assessment of AI appetite. The 17 Oct 2025 Q&A call content suggests the buyer is focused on foundational S2P automation and visibility (replace manual indirect processes, integrate CLM, gain spend analytics, enforce contract compliance) rather than advanced/agentic AI capabilities. Nikhil framed analytics as table-stakes ('the kind of intelligence that tools like yours and Coupa and Arivas provide'), not a differentiator. However, Revvity is a life-sciences/diagnostics company post-spin from PerkinElmer, an industry segment often open to AI/ML for supply-chain optimization, demand forecasting, and supplier risk. The S/4 HANA migration (pilot end 2026/Q1 2027) and the KPMG-led digital transformation (Markus's role) imply a forward-looking IT posture. The rep's 04 May 2026 invite to 'global AI summit 5/13/26' (now past, no reply) suggests Zycus sees potential AI appetite, but the buyer has not engaged. Verdict: tier is AI Curious (open to AI as a value-add if tied to concrete ROI, but not leading with it as a requirement). The 17 Oct call did not name AI, ML, or automation-intelligence as explicit criteria, but the pain points (spend visibility, analytics, maverick control) are solvable with AI and the org profile suggests receptiveness. Recommended move: test AI appetite in the next conversation by anchoring AI/Merlin to their stated pains (AI-driven tail-spend consolidation on the $50-65M unmanaged bucket, predictive contract-compliance alerts, generative supplier discovery for 2,000+ suppliers) and gauge response before making it a demo centerpiece.

best_practice_check.summary: Three substantive win-strategy levers for this deal: (1) Re-engage immediately to confirm the deal is live and S2P is still a 2026 priority — the 8-month buyer silence post-Q&A and the rep's unanswered 04 May outreach create execution risk that another vendor (likely Ariba or Coupa) has engaged more aggressively or the RFP is stalled/de-prioritized; without confirmation in the next 7 days, this deal risks slipping to 2027 or being lost quietly. (2) Activate the ERP-integration wedge vs Ariba as the centerpiece of the next conversation — Revvity's SAP ECC + Dynamics + future S/4 landscape and the 'don't wait for S/4 cutover' advice from John (17 Oct 2025) is a surgical, technical win lever that directly addresses Markus's (digital transformation lead, S/4 owner) core concern; make this the demo anchor and convert Markus into an internal advocate. (3) Map and engage the Economic Buyer within 14 days — at $350k with a $120-130M addressable spend scope, CFO or CPO sign-off is required, yet no exec has appeared; without EB identification and a direct relationship, commercials, pricing, and budget approval cannot begin, and the deal cannot progress beyond shortlist even if technically preferred.

competitive_position.summary: Competitive landscape is partially known. Two vendors were named on the 17 Oct 2025 Q&A call as S2P category peers: Coupa and SAP Ariba (Nikhil: 'tools like yours and Coupa and Arivas provide market intelligence'), but their status as active shortlist competitors is unconfirmed. No other rival has been mentioned, and the Salesforce Competitor field is empty. The single strongest current threat is inferred to be SAP Ariba (high threat level) due to Revvity's SAP ECC footprint and live S/4 HANA migration (pilot end 2026/Q1 2027), making Ariba a natural fit and likely a shortlist peer. However, Zycus positioned a pragmatic wedge on 17 Oct 2025: Srishti and John emphasized Zycus is 'one of only two S2P players with certified S/4 HANA adapters' and advised Revvity to integrate with ECC now and update adapters later when S/4 is live, contrasting this with PWC's experience ('they made us wait for S/4 cutover, don't recommend that'). Coupa is a secondary peer threat (medium level) based on brand and Nikhil's neutral mention, but no evidence of active engagement. The 8-month buyer silence since the Q&A call raises the risk that another vendor (Ariba, Coupa, or an unnamed rival) has engaged more aggressively, or that the RFP is stalled. The shortlist size, demo schedule, and Zycus's standing vs competitors are all unconfirmed. Recommended move: re-engage Nikhil or Kevin to confirm shortlist composition, Zycus positioning, and any competitive intel on what Ariba/Coupa are emphasizing.

competitors.how_we_win: Pragmatic ERP integration flexibility wedge. Position Zycus as the adaptive choice: certified S/4 HANA adapters, but integrate with ECC and Dynamics 365 NOW (live today) and seamlessly update to S/4 later when Revvity's pilot completes (end 2026/Q1 2027), avoiding the risk of waiting for an ERP cutover that 'almost never hits the deadline' (John, 17 Oct 2025). Contrast this with Ariba's tightly-coupled SAP approach that may force Revvity to delay S2P until S/4 is live, losing 12-18 months of value on the $50-65M unmanaged indirect spend. Emphasize PWC case study (implemented Zycus with ECC, then updated to S/4) and Revvity's own multi-ERP complexity (ECC + Dynamics + future S/4) as proof Zycus is the only vendor with the breadth to handle this landscape. Make the S/4-integration roadmap and adapter certification a demo centerpiece, and secure Markus (digital transformation lead, S/4 owner) as an internal advocate for this approach.

competitors.how_we_win: Supplier collaboration scale and contract-compliance integration wedge. Revvity disclosed 2,000+ addressable suppliers, with inventory visibility needed for top 10-50 direct suppliers, plus Agiloft CLM integration to enforce contract compliance at PO time (preventing maverick buying on the $50-65M unmanaged indirect spend). Position Zycus supplier portal as purpose-built for this scale and complexity (forecast sharing, inventory upload/API, PO/catalog mgmt, all on one portal), and emphasize the Agiloft integration as a day-1 capability (Srishti confirmed feasibility on 17 Oct call). If Coupa's CLM-integration story is weaker or their supplier portal is less mature at scale, make this the wedge. Also leverage Nikhil's prior Zycus implementation experience ('I implemented Zycus in an early org, fairly proud of it' — 17 Oct 2025) to build trust and familiarity vs Coupa as a newer relationship. Activate Nikhil as a champion by reminding him of his own positive Zycus experience and contrasting it with Coupa's approach.

ai_positioning_strength.summary: AI/analytics capability (ANA product) is in scope per Salesforce Products field (eProcurement;ANA), but AI positioning was not discussed on the 17 Oct 2025 Q&A call and is under-positioned. The buyer's articulated pain points (spend visibility, analytics, maverick-spend control, market intelligence) are foundational BI/analytics use cases, not agentic AI. Nikhil mentioned 'the kind of intelligence that tools like yours and Coupa and Arivas provide in terms of market intelligence' (17 Oct 2025), framing analytics as table-stakes category capability, not a Zycus differentiator. The rep's 04 May 2026 Next Step references an 'invitation to global AI summit 5/13/26' (now ~6 weeks past), implying Zycus is trying to position AI/Merlin, but there is no evidence the buyer engaged or that the AI narrative landed. With AIS fields null (no AIS score/status/why in Salesforce), there is no signal of AI appetite from prior rep assessment. The 17 Oct call content suggests the buyer is focused on foundational S2P automation (replace manual processes, gain visibility, integrate CLM) rather than advanced AI (agentic negotiation, predictive analytics, generative sourcing). Risk: if Coupa or Ariba are positioning AI/ML-driven spend analytics or predictive sourcing as differentiators, and Zycus has not, we may be perceived as a legacy/baseline S2P player. Recommended move: in the next conversation, introduce the Merlin/I2O narrative tailored to Revvity's pain (e.g. AI-driven tail-spend analytics to surface the $50-65M unmanaged bucket, predictive contract-compliance alerts to reduce maverick buying, generative supplier discovery for the 2,000+ supplier base), and invite Nikhil and Markus to a live AI capability session (not a generic summit) before the demo, within 21 days.

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Revvity's stated must-haves from the 17 Oct 2025 Q&A call are well-aligned with Zycus capabilities, with one unresolved complexity (supplier inventory visibility via API). Strong fit: (1) ERP integration flexibility — Zycus confirmed certified S/4 HANA adapters and positioned pragmatic ECC-first integration with Dynamics 365 and future S/4 update, directly addressing Revvity's multi-ERP landscape (ECC, Dynamics, S/4 pilot end 2026/Q1 2027); (2) supplier collaboration at scale (2,000+ suppliers) — Zycus confirmed supplier portal with forecast sharing, PO/catalog mgmt, and manual inventory upload as baseline, addressing the buyer's ask for 'everything at one place' (Madhil, 17 Oct 2025); (3) Agiloft CLM integration for contract compliance at PO time — Srishti confirmed feasibility on the call, aligning with Nikhil's #1 KPI (control chaotic spend); (4) modular licensing by functionality — Markus asked for this explicitly to 'make an informed decision what makes sense for Revvity to implement', and Zycus offers modular eProcurement + Analytics (ANA, in scope per SF Products). Partial fit / open question: supplier inventory visibility via API for top 10-50 direct suppliers. Srishti flagged on 17 Oct that this depends on supplier API availability and willingness to share data, and offered manual portal upload as an alternative. Devika committed to 'check and get back' on feasibility, but no follow-up is recorded. Markus accepted the manual fallback ('as long as there's an option for them to update manually, that would be a win'), so this is not a blocker but needs confirmation. Exposed / gap: implementation cost estimate. John (Zycus VP) stated on 17 Oct that an accurate estimate requires a '3-party meeting (Zycus, SI partner, Revvity) to workshop it… throwing out a number now would be wildly inaccurate', but Markus said 'I still think we might need an implementation cost estimate as part of the RFP'. This was left unresolved on the call, and no 3-party meeting or cost estimate is recorded since. This is a commercial/process exposure that may block RFP progression if Revvity's procurement process requires a cost envelope to advance.

• On the Oct 17 discovery call with Markus Vogel, Arun Pandey and Nikhil Sahasrabuddhe (31 minutes), neither AI, Merlin, GenAI nor autonomous negotiation came up once from the buyer side. Vogel kept the conversation on integration, telling Zycus the platform has to work with "ECC, S4 HANA and Dynamics 365" simultaneously. The priorities were supplier inventory visibility, indirect spend governance, and implementation cost.
• Action items reinforce a classical evaluation: API feasibility, supplier portal questions, RFP timeline flexibility, and implementation partner pricing ranges. No AI champion has surfaced. With one substantive call on the books and the buyer parked at 'Don't Know' on AI, this is a wait-and-see posture rather than a Resistant deal, but there is nothing yet to lift it above the lower band.
